text: The surgeon opens the peritoneum on the right side, revealing the anatomical position of the port used for the procedure. The initial step involves locating the bladder vessels.

The surgeon dissects the vessels near the bladder, identifying and clipping the umbilical artery. The dissection continues laterally along the bladder towards the prostate, aiming to isolate the structures at the base of the prostate while preserving the nerves.

The surgeon avoids thermic coagulation while pushing the visceral part of the endopelvic fascia, exposing the vesicle prostatic vessels at the level of the bladder neck. The vessels are carefully cut at the base of the prostate.

The surgeon visually identifies the prostate and the anatomical boundary between the bladder and prostate. The right neurovascular bundle is dissected and freed. After cutting the bladder vessels, the surgeon moves to the ureter, located behind the umbilical artery, and clips the ureter while preserving vascularization. The Douglas pouch is opened, and the dissection of the prostate vessels is completed.

The surgeon cuts the superior vesicular artery, controlling the residual vessel, and moves the bladder and prostate to the left side. The dissection proceeds on the left side, isolating the umbilical artery and superior vesicle artery using cold cutting techniques.

The surgeon removes fat tissue from the bladder, performing an anatomical dissection. The Douglas pouch is opened, revealing the vas deferens and left ureter. The left ureter is controlled and cut, with limited dissection to preserve vascularization. A small clip is applied at the ureter's origin. The bladder is dissected posteriorly, exposing small branches on the left, including a vein from the bladder and prostate leading to the dorsal vein complex. Clips of ten or five millimeters are used.

The surgeon demonstrates the complete posterior dissection of the bladder, highlighting the anatomical structures such as the left vas deferens and the endopelvic fascia. The posterior aspect of the prostate is visible, along with the Denonvilliers fascia. The surgeon proceeds to dissect anteriorly after ensuring the bladder and prostate are fully dissected for a clear operating field.

The surgeon identifies the prostate, bladder, and dorsal vein complex, noting the need to cut the complex while preserving the anterior fixation of the urethra. The muscles from the bladder neck to the pubis bone, referred to as the aprons, are visible. The fascia is pushed close to the apex of the prostate to aid in preserving continence. The dorsal vein complex is not bleeding due to the pressure from the laparoscopic gas. The surgeon dissects the apex of the prostate laterally and posteriorly, pushing the endopelvic fascia to reach the apex. The catheter is removed, revealing the urethra, with a focus on preserving its length. Minor bleeding is observed.

The surgeon cuts the urethra at the level of the verru montanum, completing the cystectomy. The assistant places a name block at the apex of the prostate. The surgical field reveals the left ureter, umbilical artery, and main vessels. The anterior wall of the vagina and uterus are preserved, with a double G stent visible in the left ureter due to previous tumor resections.

The surgeon identifies the main anatomical landmarks, including the umbilical artery and ureter, and performs a lateral dissection of the bladder. The umbilical and superior vesicle arteries are visible on the right side. A cold dissection technique is used to preserve vascularization. The round ligament is identified, and dissection begins between the bladder and uterus on the left side. The iliac and umbilical arteries are clipped again for clear visualization of the bladder vessels.

The surgeon dissects between the bladder and uterus, using a metallic valve in the vagina for clear visualization. The dissection progresses medially under the bladder, preserving the lateral structures of the vagina. The inferior bladder artery is visible under the bladder neck.

The surgeon carefully preserves the first part of the urethra, along with its vascularization and innervation at the bladder neck, proceeding millimeter by millimeter using clips. The lateral face of the bladder is dissected, avoiding thermic regulation. The anterior aspect and vessels are visible, with the dissection occurring just under the bladder neck. The surgeon clips all lateral vessels, following the vagina under the balloon, and cuts at the junction between the bladder and urethra, with the dorsal vein complex and uterus visible.

The surgeon inflates the balloon to 5 cc and performs a cold dissection at the bladder neck and first part of the urethra. A clip is placed at the bladder neck, and the urethra is cut while protecting surrounding nerves and muscles. The bladder is removed following a left node dissection, and the anterior wall of the vagina is opened. The urethra is visible, and the vagina is closed to prevent fistulas. The surgeon constructs a neobladder using an ileal loop, suturing it to the urethra with a rolling suture, and performs a lateral-lateral anastomosis using a stapler.

The surgeon opens the neobladder on the anti-mesenteric area, creating a V-shaped incision. Using a V-lock 3-0 suture, the posterior part of the bladder is closed with tension applied every two passages. The anterior reconstruction of the urethra is performed over two centimeters. The neobladder is shaped with two branches resembling a heart, with the superior part of the posterior layer coming anteriorly. The Prograsp instrument is used to hold the left part of the bladder, and a needle is used to close the left part completely. The ureters are implanted in front of the neobladder without crossing the urethra, and the water tightness of the bladder is tested.

The surgeon removes the ureter from behind the iliac artery and cuts it. The neobladder is opened, and a triangular needle with monocryl 5-0 is used for suturing without tension. The posterior layer of the neobladder is visible, and the ureteric implantation is performed without stents. The surgeon tests the quality of the stitches multiple times during the procedure.

The surgeon uses a WG eight French catheter to test the water tightness of the ureteric implantation, ensuring there are no leaks. The final position of the neobladder is shown, with careful preservation of the ureter's vascularization.
